text.title: 7Ps Ideation and Paul Graham Questions
text.summary: Two complementary idea-generation frameworks: 7Ps systematic ideation and Paul Graham's introspection prompts, with anti-duplication rules for agent use.

rule.statement: Apply all 7 Ps systematically, generate 2-3 ideas per P, and tag each idea with its underlying user-pain-id to prevent duplication across Pain/Profession/People cells.
rule.rationale: Agents fill Pain/Profession/People with restatements of the same idea. Requiring a unique user-pain-id per idea forces structural diversity across the seven prompts.

list.item: Pain: What frustrates you (or your target user) daily? Focus on problems you personally understand.
Passion: What do you love doing? Look for intersection with a market need.
Profession: What is broken in your industry? Look for manual, error-prone, or expensive processes.
Process: What workflow is inefficient? Code review, billing, scheduling, onboarding.
Platform: What can be improved on an existing platform? Better Slack integrations, Notion plugins, VS Code extensions.
People: Who do you know with problems? Freelancers, founders, ops teams, developers.
Product: What product do you wish existed? What do you hack together repeatedly?

example.note: Intersection of multiple Ps is the highest-signal zone: Pain (meeting coordination is broken) + Passion (developer tooling) + Platform (VS Code) = async standup bot for distributed engineering teams.

rule.statement: For agent runs, skip "What do you find yourself building for yourself?" — agents cannot honestly answer this and will invent a project. Use the other three questions and pipe in real user history if available.
rule.rationale: Paul Graham questions reward introspection, not aggregation. LLMs trained on generic startup content will produce plausible but fabricated answers to introspective prompts.

list.item: What is tedious but necessary? Look for manual, repetitive tasks that users accept as unavoidable.
What is surprisingly hard to do? Tasks that seem simple but require expert knowledge or coordination.
What would you pay for that does not exist? Unmet needs with demonstrated willingness to pay.

rule.statement: Validate each Paul Graham answer with 2-3 other people before treating it as a market signal, and check whether solutions already exist.
rule.rationale: Personal pain points are necessary but not sufficient for a market. A solution that already exists at reasonable cost means the problem is solved, not an opportunity.
